evals.prompt: Design a culvert for cross-drainage on a Paved I road in Uganda. The design flow from Skill #29 is Q = 1.8 m3/s for a 25-year ARI. The embankment height is 3.0 m (allowable headwater = 2.5 m). The culvert length is 10 m, slope is 2%, and tailwater depth is 0.5 m. Use a concrete pipe culvert with headwall entrance. Check both inlet and outlet control to determine the governing headwater condition.
evals.expected_output: Trial 900 mm pipe: inlet control HW/D approximately 1.6, HW_i = 1.44 m. Outlet control calculation using Eqn 9.6 gives HW_oi approximately 1.31 m. Inlet control governs (1.44 > 1.31) but HW/D = 1.6 exceeds 1.5 limit. Try 1200 mm pipe: inlet control HW/D approximately 0.9, HW_i = 1.08 m. Outlet control HW_oi approximately 0.88 m. Inlet control governs at 1.08 m. HW/D = 0.9, within limits. HW_c = 1.08 m < 2.5 m allowable. Outlet velocity approximately 1.6 m/s. Use 1200 mm concrete pipe.

assertions.text: Checks BOTH inlet control and outlet control headwater (not just one)
Uses the HIGHER of inlet and outlet control headwater as the governing condition
Reports HW/D ratio and checks against the 1.2-1.5 limit
Minimum culvert diameter of 600 mm noted or applied per MoWT Section 9.10
Outlet velocity calculated and reported for erosion protection assessment

evals.prompt: Size a highway culvert in the UK for a design flow of 1.8 m3/s. The embankment height is 3.0 m and the culvert length is 10 m with 2% slope. Apply UK design requirements including CIRIA C689 principles.
evals.expected_output: Same hydraulic principles as Uganda (inlet/outlet control). UK requires 1 in 100 year + climate change. CIRIA C689 is the primary UK reference. Same dual control check procedure. Environmental considerations including fish passage should be noted. CG 501/CIRIA C689 not available locally -- flagged for engineer review.

assertions.text: References CIRIA C689 or DMRB for UK culvert design (not MoWT)
Applies same inlet/outlet control dual check procedure (same hydraulic principles)
Notes environmental considerations (fish passage) relevant to UK practice

evals.prompt: A culvert in Uganda has a design flow of Q = 0.3 m3/s with a very flat gradient of 0.3% and a long barrel length of 30 m. The embankment height is 2.0 m. The culvert is a 600 mm diameter concrete pipe with headwall entrance (Ke = 0.5, n = 0.012). Calculate both inlet control and outlet control headwater. The tailwater is 0.2 m. Identify which control condition governs and explain why.
evals.expected_output: This scenario is designed to test Pitfall #5: inlet and outlet control give DIFFERENT results. For 600 mm pipe at 0.3 m3/s: Inlet control HW/D from nomograph is moderate. Outlet control: long culvert (30 m) and flat slope (0.3%) means high friction losses and minimal elevation drop (So*L = 0.003*30 = 0.09 m). H will be significant due to the long barrel. HW_oi = H + ho - So*L will be HIGHER than HW_i because of the long barrel and flat slope. Outlet control governs. This is exactly the scenario where checking only inlet control would undersize the culvert.

assertions.text: Calculates BOTH inlet control headwater AND outlet control headwater with different numerical results
Correctly identifies outlet control as governing for this long, flat culvert scenario
Explains WHY outlet control governs: long barrel length and flat slope increase friction losses
Uses the higher headwater value as the design headwater (max of inlet and outlet)
Notes that this scenario demonstrates why BOTH controls must always be checked (Pitfall #5)

evals.prompt: Size a pipe culvert for a Class C road crossing in Kenya. The design flow is 1.8 m3/s for the appropriate return period. The culvert length is 12m with an invert slope of 1.5%. Available pipe diameters are 600mm, 900mm, and 1200mm. The embankment height is 3.5m and tailwater depth is 0.6m. Use the Kenya RDM 2.2 design procedure.
evals.expected_output: From RDM 2.1 Table 4.1, pipe culverts require 10-year design frequency. Using RDM 2.2 Ch 4 procedure: check both inlet and outlet control. For 900mm pipe: inlet control HW/D approximately 1.6, exceeds 1.5 limit. For 1200mm pipe: inlet control HW/D approximately 0.85 (within limits), outlet control HW approximately 0.9m. Inlet control governs at HW = 1.02m. Select 1200mm diameter. Reference design charts B.5-B.7 or empirical formulae (Section 4.6). Outlet velocity = 1.59 m/s -- check erosion protection needs.

assertions.text: References RDM 2.2 Ch 4 for Kenya culvert design procedure (not MoWT Vol 2 Section 9)
Checks both inlet and outlet control and identifies which governs
References design charts from Appendix B (nomographs) or empirical formulae from Section 4.6
Selects appropriate diameter (1200mm) that satisfies HW/D limits
Notes outlet velocity and references erosion protection assessment (Skill #32)

evals.prompt: Size a culvert for a DC7 trunk road in Ethiopia crossing a seasonal stream. The design flood (25-year return period) is 8 m3/s. The road embankment height is 4 m. The stream has a steep gradient (2%) and carries significant sediment during the wet season. Using the ERA Drainage Design Manual 2013, determine the culvert type, size, and check both inlet and outlet control headwater. Specify material based on ERA practice.
evals.expected_output: Per ERA DDM 2013: dual-control approach checking both inlet and outlet control (FHWA/AASHTO methodology). For 8 m3/s with 4 m embankment: reinforced concrete box or pipe culvert. Size determined by allowable headwater ratio (HW/D). ERA prefers reinforced concrete pipe (RCP), reinforced concrete box, and stone masonry as culvert materials. Check both inlet and outlet control; higher headwater governs. Sediment management critical for Ethiopian seasonal streams.

assertions.text: References ERA Drainage Design Manual 2013 for culvert hydraulic design (not Uganda MoWT or Kenya RDM)
Checks both inlet control and outlet control per ERA DDM dual-control approach
Specifies ERA-preferred culvert materials (RCP, RC box, or stone masonry)
Addresses sediment considerations for Ethiopian seasonal stream conditions
